real_meaning: ～ところ literally means 'place' but when attached to verb forms it captures a specific snapshot in time — it tells the listener exactly WHERE in the timeline of an action you are: just about to, right in the middle of, or just finished. Think of it like a camera freeze-frame: you're not describing the action in general, you're pinpointing the precise moment. The form of the verb before ところ is doing all the temporal work.

formation.rule: Attach ところ to three different verb forms to get three different timings: (1) Dictionary form + ところ → just about to do [<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べるところ = just about to eat]; (2) ている + ところ → right in the middle of doing [<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べているところ = in the middle of eating]; (3) た (past plain) + ところ → just finished doing [<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べたところ = just finished eating].
formation.common_mistake: Learners often use the dictionary form when they mean 'just finished,' saying ×<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べるところだった instead of ○<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べたところだった. Remember: た-form = past completion, dictionary form = imminent future.

sentences.jp: <ruby>今<rt>いま</rt></ruby>から<ruby>出<rt>で</rt></ruby>かけるところだよ。
sentences.translation: I'm just about to head out.
sentences.notes: Dictionary form + ところ signals the action hasn't started yet — perfect for 'bad timing' moments like answering the door right before leaving.

sentences.jp: すみません、ただいま<ruby>確認<rt>かくにん</rt></ruby>しているところです。
sentences.translation: I'm sorry, I'm in the middle of checking on that right now.
sentences.notes: ている + ところです is common in service or workplace contexts to explain you're actively handling something at this very moment.

sentences.jp: <ruby>現在<rt>げんざい</rt></ruby>、<ruby>担当者<rt>たんとうしゃ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>資料<rt>しりょう</rt></ruby>を<ruby>作成<rt>さくせい</rt></ruby>したところでございます。
sentences.translation: The person in charge has just finished preparing the documents.
sentences.notes: た + ところ in formal/written contexts emphasizes the freshness of the completion — the documents are hot off the press, so to speak.

sentences.jp: <ruby>今日<rt>きょう</rt></ruby>こそ<ruby>告白<rt>こくはく</rt></ruby>しようとしていたところに、あいつが<ruby>現<rt>あらわ</rt></ruby>れた。
sentences.translation: Right at the moment when I was finally about to confess, that guy showed up.
sentences.notes: ところに (not just ところ alone) signals that an interruption or new development occurred at exactly that moment — this extension is a natural next step beyond basic ところ usage and often carries dramatic or frustrated feeling.

confused_with.contrast: Both ～たところ and ～たばかり mean something like 'just did X,' but they emphasize different things. ～たところ is about objective temporal closeness — the action literally just happened seconds or moments ago, often with a sense of 'and the result is right here now.' ～たばかり is more subjective and emotional — it stresses that not enough time has passed yet, often with a nuance of 'already?!' or mild protest. Compare: <ruby>着<rt>つ</rt></ruby>いたところです (I've just arrived — stating the fresh fact) vs. <ruby>着<rt>つ</rt></ruby>いたばかりです (I only just arrived — implying 'give me a break, it's too soon to ask anything of me').

bunpro_tip: Drill the three forms as a trio every time: る→ている→た maps perfectly to before→during→after. When you see a ところ sentence on BunPro, ask yourself first: 'Which freeze-frame is this?' — the verb form before ところ will answer the question for you.
